speaker: interviewee
text: -- Constantly. I was watching the film. Actually, the film was better than I originally thought. I though it was-- should I-- OK, you got things going?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. It's-- it's the-- the film isn't running. But what were you saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Randy dropped off the video and I shoved it in my VCR. And I see my picture, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take it down?

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 17th, 1996. Irvin Miller. Interviewer, Pam McKenna, in Columbus, Ohio. The interview is in English. OK. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello. I'm Pamela McKenna. Today I have the privilege of interviewing Mr. Irvin Miller on Sunday, November 17th, 1996. We are in Columbus, Ohio, in the United States, and the interview will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Begin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Miller, would you tell me your full name that was given to you at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name-- full name would be Iceek Miller, or Ichock. It comes from the Hebrew. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How would that be spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: --miller. Ichock, you asked me it very quickly, because I never spelled my name, Ichock. I-- it's I-C-H-O-C-K, Ichock, but they called me Iceek, would be I-C-E-E-K. Miller, M-I-L-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And growing up, did you ever go by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about later in your life? Have you ever gone by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Except the Irvin, I was named by my cousins. I wanted my middle name, which would be Jacob. She said, we have too many Jacobs in our family, so Irwin. I couldn't even pronounce it. It started with Irwin, and then, finally it became Irvin, because I couldn't pronounce the W. So I'm Irvin or Irv from then on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jacob was your middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Targowica, Poland, just a small townlet in eastern Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell Targowica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Targowica was spelled T-A-R-G-O-W-I-C-A. It's-- if you translate it, it's like 'commercial point,' what it meant. It was an interesting little town with hills and two rivers.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born July 12, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Edel. My mother's name was Chana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's maiden name was Pressman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a minute ago, you were talking about the town. It was very small and it had two rivers. What else can you tell me about the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town-- from my dad used to talk about it and my mom, constantly the wars. Even before World War I, there was always battles. It's kind of a strategic area, because of the hills and the rivers. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in-- were in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was certain how many. I would guess about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] probably 300 families, Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a mostly Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be an educated guess. There were some Poles and there was some Ukrainians, but mostly Jewish. We had two synagogues. And there was two churches, the Catholic church and a-- and a Russian Orthodox Church. Actually, I don't know if it's Ukrainian Orthodox. Mostly Ukrainians were going to that church.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother, was-- we called him Schulich, Israel. And my second brother was Abraham. And my third brother was Zindel. And then was me, Iccek, and my daughter, Rose. And late in the life of my parents, right before the war, a little brother called Benzion was born. And I know my oldest brother was 20 when my little brother was born, so it was a surprise. I think my parents were not very happy about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have extended family near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Right near us was my uncle and aunt and two cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's side of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my mother's brother, Uncle Meir, and Aunt Perl and two cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I don't recall my grandparents. I mean, they left-- two of my grandparents left for the United States way before I was born. They came here in 1922. And my father's mother died in our house. My-- and I was talking to my brother, and I said, I think I remember an old lady. And it's just like a dream. I'm not certain about it. He says, well, he says, my-- you know, our grandmother lived with us. But nothing else I remember, when she died or anything like that. But I thought I do remember. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I must have been, I don't know, maybe three years old or two years old. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which grandparents emigrated to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where did they emigrate to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to Columbus. They lived in Columbus. And we also had two of my uncles and two aunts migrated, in the year-- I think my grandparents came in '22 with my two aunts. The uncles came before that. A great uncle came before the 19th century. And he brought, slowly, one at a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family like life-- your family life like growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as a child, as a young kid, I remember we had some good times. We used to play a lot. We had a lot of little friends. We were constantly outside, always playing games and doing some things. It wasn't an unhappy childhood. I was happy. My parents were wonderful people. And the brothers and-- we got along. I was always proud of my older brothers. And it's just unfortunate that it ended so soon, at the age of 12, things-- or the age of 10, the war in '39 started, and things were never the same from then on. So up to 10 years old, what I remember was just fine. We were not-- we were poor people. I mean, by the standard of that little town, we were not, because there were a lot of people had a lot less than we did, so we considered ourselves fortunate that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of games did you play outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- one of the games I remember was a lot like baseball, but we used a small rubber ball, almost like a racquetball. And we used sticks instead of bats. And it was very close to baseball. And there's other little games. In summertime we would just-- did different things. We played soccer. We never had a good ball in soccer, so we made our own. And there was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my brothers played volleyball. Winter sports was fun, because we're right near hills. We did some homemade skis, did some skiing, homemade skates. Right by the river, so we did some skating. And I did also a little fishing. I love fishing, because that was so close. That was about, probably, 200 yards to the river, maybe less. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make your skis and your skates?

speaker: interviewee
text: The skis, we-- we were-- there was a-- a blacksmith shop. And I always used to hang out there, always tried to help them. So I would get things done.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he made some skis, shaped them out of wood. And it was no complicated thing. You put a strap and all we had to do is just move our shoe into it, and that was it. Skates was also made out of wood, and used a wrought iron on the outside to-- where we'd do the sliding and sledding and-- and drill-- not drill holes, but burn holes where you put strings and tie around our shoes. And there was our homemade ice skates. And did you make your own fishing pole, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We made our own fishing pole. [SIGHS] There was a place that we used to cut the wood down for the pole. And we would go in the market and the horses would just be in the market and we'd pull horses' hair out of their tail and able to ply, like, four strings together, and tie them up, and use a goose feather. And we would have the line go through that goose feather and put a hook, which was not expensive. I mean, for a nickel, probably-- or 5 groszy, which was worth a penny, we'd get like three hooks. And we would catch flies at home in the kitchen and put them in a-- a matchbox, . And we use that, or dough, just kind of-- or if you wanted bigger fish, we would dig worms. So there was-- we always kept busy as kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you catch a lot of fish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Used to catch little fish, and always I wanted my mom to make those, fry them, because I loved them. And there were times that she did it. She was always so busy, you know, feeding such a big family. But at times, they were too little to mess with. But she used to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your house like?

speaker: interviewee
text: House was kind of a-- it's all one floor. We lived like by the street, by a-- by a road that used to go to the police station. And to the-- there was also a public bath. People-- It's a big house. It was spread out. We had a picket fence around it. We also have attached barns, where my dad would keep the horses. And-- and it had a huge-- we call it veranda. It's like a porch facing the river. It was very interesting. And-- and we used to play underneath that. We used to play under the bridge for some reason. I don't know. There was always mysterious things happening under the bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the barn attached to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a barn attached to-- yes, to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else live in the house besides your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We rented part of the house to some other people. One was a veterinarian. And then later on, there was someone else. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a-- dealt with horses, used to buy and sell horses. He traded horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it his own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was his business, yes. He traveled just about every day to the markets. And they were just-- not in our little town. There were-- were none. But some of the major cities he would travel to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he trade horses for farmers, or for racing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did for all kinds of horses. He had some-- occasionally would have racing horses, show horses, and also to some farmers, and mostly farmers who were very large farmers, that they-- you know. Small farmers always somehow managed to raise their own, or something, just like-- every small farmer would have two horses. But some of these huge, huge, farms, they'd work mostly with horses. And he would supply them and then buy some back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ride horses at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used to ride horses. We all-- we all did, bareback. I must have ridden horses the age of three or four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father teach you about horses? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My dad, actually, during the First World War, he was a cavalryman, so he used to-- people used to admire watching my dad riding horses. He was just very good at it. I guess he was the mailman, at one point. He delivered mail. And we used to always watch-- enjoy watching dad. And I don't know whether he taught us. He was too busy doing things. But we learned on our own. My brother, he was extremely good. He was sort of a daredevil. He tried horses that everybody was scared to come near them. He would try to ride them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Abraham. He was a daredevil of all kinds. He would jump from the bridge into the water. He would walk on the ice when it was like a half an inch thick, and it would crack under his-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was quite a guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many horses would your father have at one time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I remember there was some seven, eight horses. But there was a place that he would have somewhere else that he would keep horses. But in our barn was at the most I think, maybe seven or eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any chores that you had to do around the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did a lot. In fact, I did most of them without Dad asking me. I always-- it seemed like I always tried to please my dad, and I was a hard-working kid. I used to bring water from the river, fill up the barrels, feed the horses, feed the cow. We had a-- we had a cow. And so, always worked. I just always remember working. I think I worked harder than my older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were religious holidays like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were always great. They were just always wonderful, because the food was so good and different and a lot. And it was just great. I have good memories about Passover was a wonderful holiday, or any holiday, because-- I guess, mostly for the food. We did go-- of course, we all went to synagogue. And I guess Dad would stay home. It was a nice-- you know, we did some things with him. [SIGHS] And yeah, some good memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Passover, because there was so much good food. And-- and it lasted so long, seven days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a certain point in your childhood when you realized that there was anti-Semitism around you, or was it something that you remember always being there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was always-- we were always reminded of. We just kind of knew about it. I don't think it was-- we lived mostly among Jewish people, but there were some Gentiles. And on occasions, we would play with some of the Gentile kids. And then occasionally there would arise the [NON-ENGLISH], Jew to Palestine, things like that. But this was not ongoing. I mean, it happened on occasions. We fought back. We had battles. Kids were fighting with each other, and then we made up. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] At that time, it wasn't-- it wasn't bad, as far as I can remember. But we knew there was anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, if you-- the war, I remember also in 1939, when the war-- let's see. 1939 was the pact between Stalin and Hitler. And the Russians marched in in 1939 to our part of the country. And-- and the war actually started in-- with the-- Russia and Germany in 1941. And this was June the 22nd. I remember very vividly that day, yes. I was-- it was on a Sunday. We were gathered in a-- in school for was some important official supposed to make a speech. And they were interrupted by announcement that a war started. Actually, in fact, there was a song written in-- right-- during the War. [INAUDIBLE]. Exactly at 4 o'clock, you know, I remember this song at one time. So that date is kind of-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were gathered in the school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for a reason other than--

speaker: interviewee
text: For reason-- I mean, this was on a Sunday, which there was no school. But it was some kind of a commissar was supposed to come in. And-- and there was an important meeting. And I was, at that time, a Pioneer, and I think I had a-- I was the head of a small group, like 12 kids. And then the-- then the next thing was the Komsomol, and others. So-- and I don't remember what the speech was about. But like I said, it was quickly interrupted, because of the-- in the afternoon, Sunday afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pioneer was the name of the group of boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pioneer was like Boy Scouts. You know, you-- but-- but it's-- it's a little different. I think the Russian indoctrination, you start with the Pioneers and the Komsomol, and then maybe later on, the Communist Party. So-- but we-- you know, like Boy Scouts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the announcement came, what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we dispersed and called off. And-- and from then on, there was no more school. And within a very short time, within maybe a week, the Germans already went through our part of the-- our little town. At that time, when they were coming in, maybe a day before, two days, my brother Abraham, my dad insisted that he runs further east, not to stay here, because it was dangerous. They thought, you know, if anything, they're going to do harm to young-- young men, especially of army age. My oldest brother was already in the Russian army. He was in officers' training school. So he went in 1940, just a year before the war started. So after that, we-- we lost contact with him. There were no more letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war started you lost contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, when the war-- yeah. When the war started, everything was interrupted, so-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother drafted?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He volunteered. He wanted to go to become an officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is your brother Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were saying that your father wanted Abraham to-- to run?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Right. When the-- when the Germans were coming towards, they were very close, he wanted him to run, to run away. And he did. I guess he too, wanted, cause he told me years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day that he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I remember just vaguely. I do remember when he left, yes. I mean, he left so quickly. I mean, he just grabbed something. He didn't carry much with him. He just took off. I mean, it was not-- goodbye, and that was it. He took off. They were just scared. I mean, the Germans could be any-- any-- any hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you lose contact with him totally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We just-- immediately, once he left, that was-- that was it. That was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Germans come into your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some units. I remember there was-- there was-- the first I've seen was on motorcycles, and then, of course, army Jeeps and trucks. They just went through. They didn't-- they spent one or two days, occupied some of the areas, and then they just went through. And then they-- we didn't see any Germans. There were mostly Ukrainians. There were a lot of nationalistic groups who-- who hated the Russians. And they hoped that Germany would give them independence in that part of the-- And they were-- and they were actually working with the Germans, you know, hoping that that's what's going to happen, so they did a lot of dirty work for the Germans arresting Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Ukrainian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were. At the beginning, they were not soldiers. There were no soldiers. But they were young, mostly younger people. But they quickly organized. Germans gave them uniforms and some training.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a time when they came in and stayed for good?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Ukrainians were always around there. In fact, a lot of these people knew my parents, knew my dad. And there was a time when they-- some of these people would come every night-- they made us-- and get us out of the house until 1:00 in the morning, line us up in front of the wall like they're going to kill us. And they threatened my dad constantly because of-- of my two older brothers. One was in the army and the other one went with the Russians. So-- so they would do that for several-- for a couple weeks. If it's not every night, it's every other night, point the guns at us. We were scared to death. We thought every time they do that, they're going to kill us. And my dad, actually, was known as a good person. I mean, most people who knew him, I mean, Gentile, Jew or Gentile, they had a lot of respect for my dad. He was just a good person. But they threatened him only because of my brothers. They were young brothers, so it was like a-- you know, it's a sin to have-- and being in the army, I would imagine, has something to do with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't we stop the tape here?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
